title: Minor: Differentiability of root map Psi assumed without justification

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
## Gap Description

The proof of Observation obs:scores differentiates gamma((a+b)t) = Psi(alpha(at), beta(bt))
with respect to t and invokes the chain rule to obtain:
  (a+b) * gamma'(0) = Jac(a * alpha'(0), b * beta'(0))

The proof of Lemma lem:hess computes partial_t gamma_i(t) and partial_t^2 gamma_i(t)
along the path (alpha+tu, beta+tv), using the Jacobian Jac and Hessians H^{(i)} of
the root map Psi_{boxplus_n}.

## What Is Unverified

The root map Psi: R^n x R^n -> R^n, (alpha, beta) |-> ordered roots of p boxplus_n q,
is used as a C^2 map. The solution never establishes this differentiability.

## Why It Is True

At a point (alpha, beta) where p boxplus_n q has simple roots:
1. The polynomial coefficients c_k(alpha, beta) are smooth (polynomial) functions of (alpha, beta).
2. Each root gamma_i of the degree-n polynomial with smooth coefficients c_k is a simple root.
3. By the Implicit Function Theorem: if P(z, c) = sum_k c_k z^{n-k} and P(gamma_i, c(alpha,beta)) = 0
   with partial_z P(gamma_i, c) != 0 (simple root), then z_i = gamma_i is a smooth function of c,
   hence of (alpha, beta).
4. The ordering of roots is consistent locally (no crossing for simple roots), so the ordered
   root map is also smooth.

## How To Fill The Gap

Add a sentence after "Thus alpha, beta, gamma = Psi(alpha,beta) have distinct entries and are
smooth functions of the coefficients of the corresponding polynomials":
"Smoothness of gamma_i as a function of (alpha, beta) follows from the Implicit Function Theorem
applied to the equation P(gamma_i, c(alpha, beta)) = 0, since the partial derivative partial_z P
= prod_{j != i}(gamma_i - gamma_j) != 0 at simple roots."

comments.author: solver-agent
comments.body:
## Resolution

The current `solution.tex` already contains a complete and rigorous fix for this gap, exactly along the lines suggested.

**Lemma `lem:smooth`** ("smoothness of $\Psi_{\boxplus_n}$ on the simple-root locus") establishes that the ordered root map is $C^\infty$ on the simple-root locus. Its proof carries out precisely the IFT argument requested:

1. The coefficients $c_0=1,c_1,\dots,c_n$ of $p\boxplus_n q$ are polynomial (hence $C^\infty$) in $(\alpha,\beta)$, via the explicit coefficient formula / Walsh representation.
2. It defines $F(x;\alpha,\beta):=(p\boxplus_n q)(x)=\sum_k c_k(\alpha,\beta)x^{n-k}$, $C^\infty$ jointly.
3. At each simple root $x_0=\gamma_i$, $\partial_x F(x_0;\alpha_0,\beta_0)=(p\boxplus_n q)'(x_0)=\prod_{l\ne i}(x_0-\gamma_l)\neq 0$, so the Implicit Function Theorem yields a unique local $C^\infty$ root function $\gamma_i$.
4. Local consistency of the ordering (no crossing of simple roots) is handled by continuity, so the *ordered* root map equals $(\gamma_1,\dots,\gamma_n)$ and is $C^\infty$ on the simple-root locus.

The paragraph immediately following the lemma states: "Henceforth, on the simple-root locus, $\Psi_{\boxplus_n}$ is $C^\infty$ by Lemma~\ref{lem:smooth}; let $\mathsf{D}_{\boxplus_n}$ denote the Jacobian ... and write $H^{(i)}:=\operatorname{Hess}(\Psi_{\boxplus_n})_i$ ... Both exist classically and are used freely below."

This is exactly what licenses (a) the chain-rule step in Observation `obs:scores` giving $(a+b)\gamma'(0)=\mathsf{D}_{\boxplus_n}(a\alpha'(0),b\beta'(0))$, and (b) the first- and second-order Taylor expansions of $\gamma_i(t)$ in Lemma `lem:hess`.

The IFT identity flagged in the issue ($\partial_z P=\prod_{j\ne i}(\gamma_i-\gamma_j)\ne 0$ at simple roots) appears verbatim. No further edit to the proof is required; the differentiability of $\Psi_{\boxplus_n}$ is fully justified. (Note: no LaTeX toolchain is available in the workspace to recompile, but the file was not modified.)

Resolving as fixed.
